title: Project Management Methodologies and an introduction to “Jira”
desc: What is Agile Framework in Project Management ?
content:
[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/660/0*9HWpea8rqk6dkumW.png" alt="geeks for geeks — link"]

> What is Agile Framework in Project Management ?

This method was created to address all the challenges of conventional project management methods, those are related on planning and documentation. In this method we are with a little flexibility to adapt to changing the user requirements.

The Agile framework emphasizes collaboration, continues improvement and delivering value to the customer through incremental improvements and the recent feedback.

> Agile Project Management Principles.

- Prioritizing customer satisfaction through early and continuous delivery of valuable software or products.
- Embracing change and being responsive to changing requirements.
- Delivering working software or products frequently, with a preference for shorter time-frames.
- Collaborating closely with customers and stakeholders throughout the project.
- Building projects around motivated individuals and giving them the tools and support they need to succeed.
- Focusing on delivering a working product or software, rather than comprehensive project management documentation.
- Striving for excellence and continuous improvement through regular reflection and adaptation.

> Agile methodologies.

This method includes various methodologies. Such as Scrum, Kanban, Lean and others, each with its own unique set of principles and practices.

> Key elements of Agile project management.

1. Iterative planing and delivery.

Instead of planing whole project upfront, teams are planning and deliver the project in small, incremental stages or sprints. This allows you for feedback and adjustments throughout the project life-cycle. This is critical in Agile sprint planing.

2. Collaborative and self-organizing teams.

Teams are cross-functional and self-organizing, with members from different areas such as development, design, and testing. Teams work closely with the customer or product owner to ensure that the project meets the customer’s needs.

3. Continuous improvement and adaptation.

Agile Project Management emphasizes continuous improvement and adaptation, with regular retrospectives to evaluate the team’s performance and identify areas for improvement.

4. Emphasis on delivering value.

Agile Project Management focuses on delivering value to the customer through working software or other tangible outputs. This is achieved by prioritizing tasks and features based on their value to the customer.

It is a flexible and adaptive approach to project management that allows teams to deliver value to the customer in a collaborative and iterative manner.

> Benefits of Agile for Project management.

Agile project management enhances flexibility, speeds up delivery, improves quality, boosts collaboration, and increases customer satisfaction. It also promotes transparency and reduces risk, leading to more efficient, cost-effective, and successful project outcomes.

> Scrum and Kanban

Two popular Agile methodologies are Scrum and Kanban, both designed to deliver iterative value through flexibility and continuous improvement.

1. Scrum :

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1000/0*gMwCpaJnUIFBNWgq.jpg" alt="Link"]

Scrum breaks work into fixed-length iterations called “sprints” (usually 2–4 weeks) with defined roles.

- Time-boxed sprints, defined roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master, Developers), and specific ceremonies.
- Best For: Projects with rapidly changing requirements where structured, consistent feedback is needed.

2. Kanban:

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/661/0*0879QYqDQt49D01g.png" alt="Link"]

Kanban uses a visual board to manage tasks, focus on continuous flow, and limit work-in-progress.

A visual system that focuses on delivering work as it comes, emphasizing continuous improvement and efficiency.

- Kanban boards (visualizing workflow), work-in-progress (WIP) limits to prevent bottlenecks, and continuous delivery.
- Best For: Teams that need to manage continuous flows of incoming work or tasks that change priority frequently.

> Why Jira ?

Jira is widely used for project management because it helps teams plan, track, and manage tasks efficiently in one centralized platform. It supports popular methodologies like Agile and Scrum by providing features such as issue tracking, sprint planning, workflow customization, and real-time progress monitoring through dashboards and reports. Jira also improves team collaboration by making responsibilities clear, tracking project timelines, and integrating easily with other development tools, making it especially useful for software and engineering projects.

References : Comparing Agile Project Management Methodologies: Scrum vs Kanban

title: The First Step into Computer Vision - The Convolutional Classifier
desc: If You are a beginner or a pro in Computer vision, the first thing you had learnt is the “Convolutional Classifier”.
content:
If You are a beginner or a pro in Computer vision, the first thing you had learnt is the “Convolutional Classifier”.

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/971/1*EryWIJGeydBhrQKHMxLqDw.png" alt="source : Convolution Neural Network for image classification — Research Computing and Data Workshop"]

In a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), the convolutional classifier is the final part of the network that takes high-level features extracted by convolutional/pooling layers and turns them into a final classification prediction.

A convent used for image classification of two parts:

1. Convolutional Base.

2. Dense Head.

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/705/1*RDEqi33Nx_WeA9b_dAM8Kg.png" alt="parts of convents."]

The base is used to extract the features from an image. (It is formed primarily of layers performing the convolution operation, but often includes other kinds of layers as well)

The head is used to determine the class of the image.( It is formed primarily of dense layers but might include other layers like dropout.)

- What is a visual feature in a Image? — A feature could be a line, a color, a texture, a shape, a pattern — or some complicated combination.

The whole process is something like this:

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/706/1*crQKKRffnDRWSNhqFZrHoQ.png" alt="Base is extracting the features and Head is classify the images (determine the class of the image)."]

Train the classifier

The goal of training is the network to learn followings.

- Which features to extract from the image (Base).
- Which class goes with what features (head).

The modern approach is, these convents are rarely trained from scratch. We reuse the base of a pretrained model. Then we attach an untrained head to the pretrained base.

In other words: “we reuse the part of a network that has already learned to do 1. Extract features and attach to it some fresh layers to learn 2. Classify.”

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/491/1*nd-CJ3TgNfm-Fd7FvvwnZA.png" alt="The head usually consists of only a few dense layers, very accurate classifiers can be created from relatively little data."]

Reusing a pretrained model is a technique known as transfer learning. It is so effective, that almost every image classifier these days will make use of it.

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/894/1*QSKGwWWWv8-JE_H3aLl5UQ.png" alt="CNN — Introduction to Convolution Neural Network — GeeksforGeeks"]

When I learn this, it was little unclear to understand the structure of the classifier. The following explanation gives you a full understanding of the CNN structure (According to the above image of CNN).

> Input Layer — This contains our input image

> The Base model (The Feature extractor) — contains both of the convolutional and pooling layers (reduce the size of the features that are mapped).

> The Classifier Head — contains Flatten layer (Convert 2D feature maps → 1D vector), Dense layer (Hidden — Learn high-level patterns) and transform/Output layer (Convert to probability).

This is the first lesson to your computer vision journey. You can do this more Handson way in Kaggle.

“As machines learn to see, we are reminded that vision is not just about light hitting pixels — it is about understanding, meaning, and imagination. The camera may capture the world, but computer vision is teaching us how to truly see it.” — Great journey in CV.

Sources: Kaggle, geeksforgeeks, Research Computing and Data Workshop

title: Beauty of Recursion - “Tower of Hanoi “
desc: In CSE, “Recursion” is a well-known method for problem solving. In recursion, Function calls it’s self with a smaller version of the original problem. (Dream in a Dream).
content:
In CSE, “Recursion” is a well-known method for problem solving. In recursion, Function calls it’s self with a smaller version of the original problem. (Dream in a Dream).

Think of it as a process of breaking down a problem into smaller pieces until you reach a point that’s simple enough to solve directly.

In computer science students, “Tower of Hanoi” is a famous recursive algorithm. In this, I will talk about both recursive and iterative algorithms.

Think there are 3 disks on each other by descending order of the radius from bottom to top. All of these disks are on the A — rod. We want to pass all disks to C- rod using another rod, which is auxiliary, B.

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/828/1*EFGwLrAxddeEhzkp_bx-kQ.png" alt="01"]

Firstly, I send the first disk (which is the smallest), from rod-A to rod-C

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1024/1*59ZQfUXEC2p9fRnUeR48Dw.png" alt="02"]

Now, we use the auxiliary rod for green disk.

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1024/1*9nnuVcLQxorXXP4Ml4hotQ.png" alt="03"]

Now again remember the order. Now I put pink disk to rod-B. (Think if you put pink into rod-A, you will be in trouble …!)

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/931/1*zhn4NLwVIGa2gz_C91EEmg.png" alt="04"]

Afterwards put blue disk into rod-C and after pink disk into rod-A.

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/869/1*JvY6o3quOapxsx9a2IpvdQ.png" alt="05"]

Now put green disk and pink disk to rod-c respectively.

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/693/1*W6-aPletOpWdJiC7QAWMYA.png" alt="06"]

By analyzing all above cases, we can understand the base case for recursive case.

```
// Online C++ Compiler - Build, Compile and Run your C++ programs online in your favorite browser#include<iostream>using namespace std;void tower_of_hanoi(int n, char from_rod, char to_rod, char aux_rod ){        /*     rods    have all disks assending order    A - from rod    C - to rod    B- auxilary rod     n  = 3     first we use auxilary rod for smallest disk     A -----------> C     secondly we use rod B for other disk    A -----------> B     C -----------> B    A -----------> C     B -----------> A     B -----------> C     A -----------> C     */    if (n== 0){        return ;    }    tower_of_hanoi(n-1, from_rod, aux_rod, to_rod);    cout << "Disk " << n << ": " << from_rod << "--------->" << to_rod << endl;        tower_of_hanoi(n-1, aux_rod, to_rod, from_rod);    }int main(){   tower_of_hanoi(3, 'A', 'C', 'B' );        return 0;    }
```

Recursion tree for tower of Hanoi.

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1024/1*FWnxJTpU1Cv0A4MdQoBqDw.png" alt="Image"]

title: What I Know About FPGA
desc: Unlike traditional microprocessors (application specific integrated circuits), FPGAs allow hardware-level customization for different applications.
content:
### A field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) is a type of integrated circuit (IC) that can be configured or programmed by a user after manufacturing to perform specific tasks.

Unlike traditional microprocessors (application specific integrated circuits), FPGAs allow hardware-level customization for different applications.

Key Features:

1. FPGAs can be multiple times to update or modify their functionalities.
2. Parallel Processing-FPGAs can execute multiple tasks simultaneously.
3. FPGA often use to speed up computations, making them ideal for AI, cryptography, and real time processing.
4. Logic blocks interconnections-FPGAs consist of configurable logic blocks connected by programmable interconnections.

Applications:

1. Signal Processing
2. AI and ML
3. Cryptography and Cybersecurity
4. High performance computing
5. Networking and telecommunication
6. Embedded systems and Iot Devices

[IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER: src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1024/1*Y9VZ7x5cmXMu_YENqmTidA.jpeg" alt="Image"]

title: Selection Sort
desc: “Selection sort” is a comparison-based algorithm. The sorting process is going like this.
content:
“Selection sort” is a comparison-based algorithm. The sorting process is going like this.

By selecting small or largest value of the array and swap it with the first element of the array. This process continues until the array is sorted.

1. First, we find the smallest element of the array and swap it with the very first element in the array. (The smallest element is at the correct position).
2. After that, we find the smallest among rest of the array (The second smallest). Then we swap it with the second element.
3. We keep doing that, until we get all elements moved to correct position in the array.

Go and check the demonstration of the Selection sort here — Sorting (Bubble, Selection, Insertion, Merge, Quick, Counting, Radix) — VisuAlgo

Time complexities:

For best case — O(N**2)

For average case — O(N**2)

For worst case — O(N**2)

```
#include<iostream>#include<vector>using namespace std;void selection_sort(vector<int> arr){        for (int i = 0; i < arr.size()-1; i++){        int min_indx = i;                for (int j = i+1; j < arr.size(); j++){            if (arr[j] < arr[min_indx]){                min_indx = j;                            }        }        swap(arr[min_indx], arr[i]);                    }        for (int num: arr){        cout << num << " ";    }        }int main(){    vector <int> arr = {20, 10, 5, 2, 1, 100, 32};    selection_sort(arr);        }
```
